name: Museum of the Bible
address: 400 4th St SW, Washington, DC 20024, USA
description: A nondenominational museum near the National Mall dedicated to the history, narrative, and cultural impact of the Bible. Its permanent collection includes rare early Bibles and manuscripts, Dead Sea Scroll fragments, and galleries tracing the Bible's role in American history and around the world. Founded in 2017 by the evangelical Green family, owners of Hobby Lobby.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: ajay_suresh, CC BY 2.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY 2.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Billy Graham Center Museum, Wheaton College
address: 500 College Ave, Wheaton, IL 60187, USA
description: A museum on the campus of Wheaton College tracing the life and worldwide preaching ministry of evangelist Billy Graham, from his rural North Carolina upbringing through decades of evangelistic crusades broadcast around the world. Housed in Billy Graham Hall alongside the Billy Graham Center's archives of evangelical history.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: Sea Cow, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Bob Jones University Museum & Gallery
address: 1700 Wade Hampton Blvd, Greenville, SC 29614, USA
description: One of the largest collections of religious art in the United States, assembled by Bob Jones Jr. beginning in 1948 and featuring Old Master paintings on biblical and Christian themes by artists including Rubens, Botticelli, and van Dyck, alongside a smaller collection of ancient Near Eastern and biblical-era artifacts. The university itself drew scrutiny for a ban on interracial dating that remained in place until 2000.
status: Closed to walk-in visitors as of 2026 while relocating to a new site in downtown Greenville; group tours available by reservation

image_source.credit: Curt Teich & Co., Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: Public domain — no attribution required.
image_source.note: General BJU campus photo — no free photo of the museum building specifically was found.

name: Creation Museum
address: 2800 Bullittsburg Church Rd, Petersburg, KY 41080, USA
description: A museum operated by the young-earth creationist ministry Answers in Genesis, presenting the biblical account of creation, the Genesis flood, and the Fall as a framework for interpreting natural history, alongside a botanical garden and planetarium. Its young-earth reading of Genesis is a specific theological position not shared by all evangelicals, many of whom hold old-earth or non-literal views of the creation days.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: David Berkowitz, CC BY 2.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY 2.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Ark Encounter
address: 1 Ark Encounter Dr, Williamstown, KY 41097, USA
description: A themed attraction near Cincinnati built around a full-scale wooden replica of Noah's Ark, operated by the same young-earth creationist ministry as the Creation Museum, Answers in Genesis. Exhibits inside the Ark present the Genesis flood narrative; as with the Creation Museum, its young-earth position is one specific view within a wider range of evangelical positions on Genesis.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: 1990'sguy, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: ICR Discovery Center for Science & Earth History
address: 1830 Royal Ln, Dallas, TX 75229, USA
description: A museum in Dallas operated by the Institute for Creation Research presenting young-earth creationist interpretations of geology, biology, and earth history through interactive exhibits and a planetarium. Like the Creation Museum and Ark Encounter, its young-earth position is a specific theological stance not held by all evangelicals.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: 1990'sguy, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Mennonite Heritage Center
address: 565 Yoder Rd, Harleysville, PA 19438, USA
description: A museum, library, and genealogical archive in Harleysville, Pennsylvania preserving the history of Mennonite and other Anabaptist communities in southeastern Pennsylvania, including the Mary Jane Lederach Hershey Fraktur Gallery of Pennsylvania German folk art and the John L. Ruth Historical Library.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: Keizers, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.
image_source.note: Interior exhibit photo — no free exterior building photo was found.

name: Library of Congress — Gutenberg Bible
address: Thomas Jefferson Building, 101 Independence Ave SE, Washington, DC 20540, USA
description: One of only three complete vellum copies of the Gutenberg Bible in the United States is on permanent public display in the Library of Congress's Thomas Jefferson Building — the first substantial book printed from movable type in the West, circa 1455.
interest_note: A secular national library, not a Christian institution, but its free, permanent Gutenberg Bible display is a landmark for anyone interested in the history of how the Bible came to be printed and widely distributed.
status: Open to the public, free admission

image_source.credit: Carol M. Highsmith, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: Public domain — no attribution required.
image_source.note: General Library of Congress reading room — no free photo of the Gutenberg Bible display case specifically was found.

name: Morgan Library & Museum
address: 225 Madison Ave, New York, NY 10016, USA
description: A research library and museum in New York founded on financier J. Pierpont Morgan's personal collection. It holds three copies of the Gutenberg Bible — more than any other institution in the world — plus one of the finest collections of medieval and Renaissance illuminated manuscripts in the United States, including numerous Books of Hours and biblical texts.
interest_note: A secular institution, but its manuscript holdings are an unmatched resource for the history of the Bible's transmission and medieval Christian devotional life.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: Photograph by Mike Peel (www.mikepeel.net)., CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Beinecke Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Yale University
address: 121 Wall St, New Haven, CT 06511, USA
description: Yale University's rare book library, whose iconic marble-paneled building houses a Gutenberg Bible on permanent public display on its mezzanine, alongside one of the world's earliest known printed texts.
interest_note: A secular university library, freely open to visitors, with a permanently displayed Gutenberg Bible of interest to anyone studying the history of the printed Bible.
status: Open to the public, free admission

image_source.credit: ajay_suresh, CC BY 2.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY 2.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: New York Public Library — Polonsky Exhibition (Gutenberg Bible)
address: Stephen A. Schwarzman Building, 476 Fifth Ave, New York, NY 10018, USA
description: The New York Public Library's flagship Stephen A. Schwarzman Building hosts the Polonsky Exhibition of the Library's Treasures, a free permanent exhibition that includes a Gutenberg Bible acquired in 1847 by library founder James Lenox, alongside other historic manuscripts and printed texts.
interest_note: A secular public library, but the permanently displayed Gutenberg Bible is of interest to anyone tracing the printed Bible's early history in America.
status: Open to the public, free admission

image_source.credit: NYC Wanderer (Kevin Eng), CC BY-SA 2.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 2.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Huntington Library
address: 1151 Oxford Rd, San Marino, CA 91108, USA
description: A research library, art museum, and botanical garden in San Marino, California, whose permanent exhibition includes one of only three vellum Gutenberg Bibles in the United States, alongside other rare early printed and manuscript Bibles.
interest_note: A secular institution, but its Gutenberg Bible and early Bible collection are of significant interest for the history of the printed Scriptures.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: Rennett Stowe from USA, CC BY 2.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY 2.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: National Museum of African American History and Culture
address: 1400 Constitution Ave NW, Washington, DC 20560, USA
description: The Smithsonian's museum on the National Mall dedicated to African American history and culture includes a permanent exhibition on the central role of Christian faith in Black American life — from the religion of the enslaved through the Black church's role in the civil rights movement — alongside artifacts such as a Bible owned by Nat Turner and items belonging to Richard Allen, founder of the African Methodist Episcopal Church.
interest_note: A secular, government-run museum, but its permanent religion exhibition is a significant resource on the history and faith of the Black church in America.
status: Open to the public, free admission (timed passes required)

image_source.credit: DestinationFearFan, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: National Civil Rights Museum
address: 450 Mulberry St, Memphis, TN 38103, USA
description: Built around the former Lorraine Motel in Memphis, where Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated on April 4, 1968, this museum traces the history of the American civil rights movement, including the room where King stayed on the night of his death.
interest_note: A secular museum of civil rights history, but a significant site for the intersection of Christian faith and public witness, given Dr. King's ministry and the movement's roots in the Black church.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: Antony-22, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: British Library — Sir John Ritblat Treasures Gallery
address: 96 Euston Rd, London NW1 2DB, England
description: The British Library's free permanent gallery of its greatest treasures, including the Lindisfarne Gospels (c. 700), a leaf of the Codex Sinaiticus — one of the two earliest surviving Christian Bibles — an original Tyndale New Testament, a Gutenberg Bible, and Handel's manuscript score for Messiah.
interest_note: A secular national library, but this single free gallery holds an unmatched concentration of landmark items in the history of the Bible's text and transmission.
status: Open to the public, free admission

image_source.credit: fsse8info from UK, CC BY-SA 2.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 2.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.
image_source.note: General British Library building — no free photo of the Treasures Gallery interior specifically was found.

name: Bodleian Libraries — Weston Library Treasury, Oxford
address: Broad St, Oxford OX1 3BG, England
description: The Bodleian Library's free permanent exhibition space in the Weston Library, showcasing rotating highlights from Oxford University's collections, among them one of the library's copies of the Gutenberg Bible.
interest_note: A secular university library; its Bible-history holdings are of interest, though the Treasury's displays rotate and a Gutenberg Bible is not guaranteed to be on view at every visit.
status: Open to the public, free admission; displays rotate

image_source.credit: Jps3, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Huguenot Museum, Rochester
address: 95 High St, Rochester, Kent ME1 1LX, England
description: Britain's only museum dedicated to the Huguenots — French Reformed Protestants who fled Catholic persecution in France after the 1685 revocation of the Edict of Nantes. Housed by the French Hospital, a charity founded in 1718 to shelter poor Huguenot refugees, the museum traces their flight, settlement in England, and lasting contribution to English trades and church life.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: ClemRutter, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Hereford Cathedral — Mappa Mundi and Chained Library
address: 5 College Cloisters, Cathedral Close, Hereford HR1 2NG, England
description: The largest surviving chained library in the world, housed at this Church of England cathedral, with some 1,500 books — including early Bibles and biblical commentaries — still attached to their medieval reading desks by chain, alongside the 13th-century Mappa Mundi.
status: Open to the public (paid entry)

image_source.credit: Diliff, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 3.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.
image_source.note: General cathedral exterior — no free photo of the Mappa Mundi & Chained Library room specifically was found.

name: International Museum of the Reformation, Geneva
address: Cour de Saint-Pierre 10, 1204 Geneva, Switzerland
description: A museum beside Geneva's Cathédrale Saint-Pierre tracing the history of the Protestant Reformation across Europe — Luther, Zwingli, Calvin, Knox, and beyond — through manuscripts, early printed Bibles, paintings, and artefacts of the reformers and the churches they shaped.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: AbrahamdeBaptista, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Gutenberg Museum, Mainz
address: Liebfrauenplatz 5, 55116 Mainz, Germany
description: A museum in Johannes Gutenberg's home city dedicated to the history of printing, holding two original copies of Gutenberg's 42-line Bible (c. 1454-55) — the first substantial book printed in the West using movable type.
interest_note: A secular museum of printing history, not a Christian institution, but its Gutenberg Bibles are foundational artefacts in the story of how the Bible came to be mass-produced and widely read.
status: Main Liebfrauenplatz building closed for rebuilding as of 2026; interim exhibition 'Gutenberg Museum MOVED' at the Natural History Museum Mainz, Reichklarastraße 1

image_source.credit: Pedelecs, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 3.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Musée du Désert, Mialet
address: Mas Soubeyran, 30140 Mialet, France
description: The largest museum of French Protestant history, in the Cévennes hamlet where Camisard leader Pierre Laporte ("Rolland") was born. It traces the 1685-1787 "Desert" period, when Huguenots worshipped in hiding after the revocation of the Edict of Nantes, through some 3,000 objects, weapons, and documents of their resistance and endurance.
status: Open to the public (seasonal hours)

image_source.credit: Daniel VILLAFRUELA., CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 3.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Museo Valdese, Torre Pellice
address: Via Beckwith 3, 10066 Torre Pellice (TO), Italy
description: The historical museum of the Waldensians — a Christian movement tracing its origins to Peter Waldo in 1174, predating and later aligning with the Reformation, and severely persecuted for centuries in the Piedmont valleys. The museum traces their history alongside a reconstruction of traditional Waldensian valley life.
status: Open to the public (seasonal hours)

image_source.credit: Mastrocom, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Melanchthonhaus, Wittenberg
address: Collegienstraße 60, 06886 Lutherstadt Wittenberg, Germany
description: The Wittenberg home of Philip Melanchthon, Martin Luther's close collaborator and the Reformation's principal systematic theologian, author of the Augsburg Confession. A UNESCO World Heritage building, its permanent exhibition traces his life, work, and lasting influence on Protestant theology and education.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: Horst Pomplun, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Husitské muzeum (Hussite Museum), Tábor
address: Žižkovo náměstí 1, 390 01 Tábor, Czech Republic
description: Housed in Tábor's Old Town Hall, this museum traces the history of the Hussite movement — the reform movement that grew out of Jan Hus's preaching and martyrdom, and whose radical wing founded Tábor itself as a fortified town in 1420. Exhibits cover Hussite theology, the Hussite Wars, and daily life among the Taborites. Broader than a single-person memorial, it covers the movement Hus's death set in motion rather than Hus's own life, which is told at his memorials in Prague and Konstanz.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: Pohled 111, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.
image_source.note: The museum occupies Tábor's historic town hall (Radnice).

name: John Rylands Research Institute and Library, Manchester
address: 150 Deansgate, Manchester M3 3EH, England
description: The University of Manchester's Victorian neo-Gothic research library. In 2025 it became the new home of Dr Williams's Library — a dissenting Protestant library founded in London in 1729 — whose collection of some 135,000 volumes and manuscripts, including the papers of Puritan pastor Richard Baxter, now makes this the world's foremost single resource for the history of English Protestant Dissent and Nonconformity.
interest_note: A secular university library, but its historic Gothic reading room is free to visit, and the Dr Williams's Library collection it now holds is an unmatched resource on English Puritan, Presbyterian, Congregationalist, and Baptist history, open to researchers and the public through the Special Collections reading room.
status: Building open to the public, free admission; Special Collections reading room access by registration

image_source.credit: Burkhard Mücke, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Christian Brethren Archive, John Rylands Library, Manchester
address: John Rylands Library, 150 Deansgate, Manchester M3 3EH, England
description: The world's largest archive of manuscripts, books, and periodicals relating to the Christian (Plymouth) Brethren movement, established in 1979 at the University of Manchester Library. Its roughly 17,000 books, pamphlets, and tracts, plus assembly and individual records, include the personal papers of biblical scholar F. F. Bruce, who taught at Manchester as Rylands Professor of Biblical Criticism and Exegesis and encouraged the archive's founding.
status: Housed within the John Rylands Library; archive access to researchers by appointment through Special Collections

image_source.credit: Burkhard Mücke, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.
image_source.note: The archive is housed inside the John Rylands Library building — same building photo as that entry.

name: Angus Library and Archive, Regent's Park College, Oxford
address: Regent's Park College, Pusey Street, Oxford OX1 2LB, England
description: A specialist archive and library of over 70,000 items documenting the history of the Baptist movement from 1612 onward, held at Regent's Park College, a permanent private hall of the University of Oxford.
status: Research visits by appointment only; photo ID and a letter of reference required

image_source.credit: Honcques Laus, CC0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC0 — no attribution required.
image_source.note: General Regent's Park College building — no free photo of the Angus Library room specifically was found.

name: Virginia Baptist Historical Society, Boatwright Memorial Library
address: 261 Richmond Way, University of Richmond, VA 23173, USA
description: A Baptist heritage archive of over 25,000 volumes and manuscript collections documenting Virginia Baptist history, including the state's role in the fight for religious liberty, housed in the Virginia Baptist Historical Society Wing of the University of Richmond's Boatwright Memorial Library.
status: Research by appointment; primary research hours 1–4pm, Monday–Friday

image_source.credit: Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: Public domain — no attribution required.
image_source.note: Boatwright Memorial Library tower, University of Richmond, where the society is housed.

name: Museum Schleitheimertal (Schleitheim Museum)
address: Kirchgasse 8, 8226 Schleitheim, Switzerland
description: A local heritage museum in the village of Schleitheim whose Anabaptist room holds one of the surviving 16th-century printed copies of the 1527 Schleitheim Confession, the foundational statement of faith drawn up nearby by Michael Sattler and other early Anabaptist leaders.
status: Open to the public on scheduled open days ("offene Sonntage"); check the museum website for current dates

image_source.credit: Rene Nueesch, CC BY 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY 3.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.
image_source.note: General village photo — no free photo of the museum building itself was found.

name: The Evangelical Library, London
address: 5/6 Gateway Mews, Bounds Green Road, London N11 2UT, England
description: Britain's leading evangelical reference and lending library, founded in 1924 by Geoffrey Williams and encouraged from 1938 by Martyn Lloyd-Jones, then an associate minister at Westminster Chapel. Holds over 80,000 volumes of evangelical theology, biography, and church history, plus archives of evangelical periodicals, available to ministers, students, and the public for reference or postal loan.
status: Open to the public, Monday–Saturday 10am–5pm, closed Sunday

name: Smithfield Martyrs' Memorial
address: St Bartholomew's Hospital, West Smithfield, London EC1A 7BE, England
description: A granite and bronze memorial erected in 1870 by the Protestant Alliance on the wall of St Bartholomew's Hospital, marking the site where John Rogers, John Bradford, John Philpot, and dozens of other Protestants — over 60 in total — were burned at the stake for their faith during the Marian persecutions of 1555–1558.
status: Open to the public, freely visible on the hospital's exterior wall

image_source.credit: Jim Osley, CC BY-SA 2.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 2.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.
image_source.note: St Bartholomew's Hospital gate at Smithfield, near the memorial plaque — no free photo of the plaque itself was found.

name: Lewes Martyrs' Memorial
address: Cliffe Hill, Lewes, East Sussex BN7 2BX, England
description: An obelisk erected in 1901 on Cliffe Hill overlooking Lewes, commemorating 17 Protestants burned at the stake in the town between 1555 and 1557 under Mary I, including Richard Woodman, who died with nine others on 22 June 1557 in the largest single burning of the Marian persecutions. Still remembered every 5 November by the town's Bonfire Societies.
status: Open to the public, outdoor monument

image_source.credit: Marathon, CC BY-SA 2.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 2.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Church Missionary Society Archive, Cadbury Research Library
address: Cadbury Research Library, Muirhead Tower, University of Birmingham, Edgbaston, Birmingham B15 2TT, England
description: The archive of the Church Missionary Society (founded 1799, now Church Mission Society) — one of the major Anglican evangelical missionary societies, whose missionaries and translators shaped the church across Africa, Asia, and the Pacific. Holds minute books, correspondence, and the letters and diaries of individual missionaries, distinct from any single missionary's personal papers held elsewhere.
status: Research visits by appointment; booking required in advance

image_source.credit: Phil Champion, CC BY-SA 2.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 2.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Bible Society Archive, Cambridge University Library
address: Cambridge University Library, West Road, Cambridge CB3 9DR, England
description: The archive of the British and Foreign Bible Society (founded 1804), whose translation and distribution work put Scripture into hundreds of languages worldwide over two centuries. Holds the Society's minutes, correspondence, registers, and financial records, deposited at Cambridge University Library; a 50-year closure period applies to the most recent records.
status: Research visits via the Manuscripts Reading Room; Cambridge University Library reader's ticket required

image_source.credit: Cambridge University Library, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.
image_source.note: Cambridge University Library, where the archive is held.

name: Keswick Convention — St John's Church, Keswick
address: St John's Church, Ambleside Road, Keswick, Cumbria CA12 4DD, England
description: St John's Church is where curate T. D. Harford-Battersby held the first Keswick Convention in 1875, a gathering for the deepening of the spiritual life that grew into an annual international convention with lasting influence on evangelical holiness teaching and the modern missionary movement. The Convention still meets each July, now largely at the nearby Keswick Convention Centre on Skiddaw Street.
status: St John's Church is a working parish church, open to visitors; the Keswick Convention Centre on Skiddaw Street is in active use only during the July convention and is not a year-round walk-in heritage site

image_source.credit: Tilman2007, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Billy Graham Center Archives, Wheaton College
address: Billy Graham Hall, 500 College Ave, Wheaton, IL 60187, USA
description: The archives department of Wheaton College's Billy Graham Center, on the third floor of Billy Graham Hall alongside the Museum. Collects unpublished records of North American evangelistic and missionary activity — the core holding is the Billy Graham Evangelistic Association's own records, alongside those of numerous other parachurch and mission organizations, broader in scope than the Museum's focus on Graham himself.
status: Research visits by appointment through Wheaton College Archives & Special Collections

image_source.credit: Sea Cow, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.
image_source.note: The archives are housed inside the Billy Graham Center — same building photo as the museum entry.

name: Southern Baptist Historical Library and Archives
address: 901 Commerce Street, Suite 400, Nashville, TN 37203, USA
description: The official historical archive of the Southern Baptist Convention, housed in the SBC Building in Nashville. Holds denominational records, association minutes, personal papers, periodicals, and other materials documenting Southern Baptist history broadly, rather than any single individual.
status: Research access; contact the archives to arrange a visit

name: Fuller Theological Seminary Archives & Special Collections
address: David Allan Hubbard Library, 135 N Oakland Ave, Pasadena, CA 91101, USA
description: The archive of Fuller Theological Seminary, documenting the broad interdenominational evangelical movement the seminary has been part of since its 1947 founding. Holds the personal papers of founder Charles E. Fuller (radio evangelist behind "The Old Fashioned Revival Hour") and longtime president David Allan Hubbard, alongside significant Holiness and Pentecostal movement collections.
status: Research access via the Hubbard Library; contact archives staff to arrange a visit

image_source.credit: Bobak Ha'Eri, CC BY 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY 3.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Pilgrim Hall Museum
address: 75 Court Street, Plymouth, MA 02360, USA
description: The oldest continuously operating public museum in the United States, founded in 1820 by the Pilgrim Society to preserve the history of the Mayflower Pilgrims' 1620 voyage and Plymouth Colony. Its collection includes William Bradford's Bible, William Brewster's chair, and other possessions of the Pilgrims — English Separatist Christians whose colony became a foundation of American church life.
interest_note: A secular historical museum run by the Pilgrim Society, not a Christian ministry, but its collection of the Pilgrims' own possessions and documents is a significant resource for the history of Separatist Christianity in early America.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: Giorgio Galeotti, CC BY 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY 4.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Whitman Mission National Historic Site
address: 328 Whitman Mission Road, Walla Walla, WA 99362, USA
description: The site of the mission station established in 1836 by Presbyterian missionaries Marcus and Narcissa Whitman among the Cayuse people, and where they and eleven others were killed in 1847 during an outbreak of measles and rising tension with Oregon Trail settlers. Now a National Park Service site with a visitor museum, trails, and monument marking the mission and gravesite.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: National Park Service, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: Public domain — no attribution required.

name: Fourah Bay College, Freetown
address: Mount Aureol, Freetown, Sierra Leone
description: Founded in 1827 by the Church Missionary Society as an Anglican missionary school, Fourah Bay College is the first Western-style institution of higher education in Sub-Saharan Africa. Established to train African teachers and clergy, its first enrolled student was Samuel Ajayi Crowther, who went on to become the first African Anglican bishop. It drew students from across British West Africa and remains part of the University of Sierra Leone today.
status: Working university campus; visits by arrangement

image_source.credit: Jared & Melanie & Huxley Ponchot, CC BY 2.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY 2.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Yanghwajin Foreign Missionary Cemetery, Seoul
address: Hapjeong-dong, Mapo-gu, Seoul, South Korea
description: A cemetery overlooking the Han River, first opened in 1890, holding roughly 376 graves — around 118 of them Protestant missionaries and their families who served in Korea from the late 19th century onward, alongside other foreign residents. It preserves the memory of the missionary generation that helped plant the Korean Protestant church, through Japanese colonial rule and the Korean War.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: Matthew smith 254, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 3.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Kuruman Moffat Mission
address: Seodin, Kuruman, Northern Cape, South Africa
description: A London Missionary Society station founded near Kuruman in 1824, made famous by Robert and Mary Moffat, who lived and worked there from 1820 to 1870. Robert Moffat completed a full Setswana translation of the Bible, printed on the mission's own press in 1857 — the first complete Bible printed anywhere in Africa, and the first in a previously unwritten African language. The site preserves the original homestead, the 1838 Moffat Church, and the mission press.
status: Open to the public as a heritage site

image_source.credit: Andrew Hall, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 3.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Cape Coast Castle
address: Cape Coast, Central Region, Ghana
description: A fortified trading post on Ghana's coast, built by the Swedish Africa Company in 1653 and later held by the British, whose underground dungeons held enslaved Africans awaiting transport across the Atlantic. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1979, restored in the 1990s and now run as a museum by the Ghana Museums and Monuments Board.
interest_note: A secular, government-run heritage site rather than a Christian institution, but a place of profound significance for anyone reckoning with the church's mixed record on slavery — the very trade that figures like John Newton profited from before his conversion, and that William Wilberforce and the Clapham Sect spent their lives working to abolish.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: Rjruiziii, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 3.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: HCJB — Voice of the Andes
address: Oe2 Vozandes N39-85 y Juan Diguja, Quito, Ecuador
description: Founded in 1931 by missionaries Clarence W. Jones, Reuben Larson, and D. Stuart Clark, HCJB was the world's first Christian missionary radio station, broadcasting its inaugural program from the Jones family's living room on a 200-watt transmitter. Its shortwave service, once heard across the globe from a transmitter site near Pifo, ended in 2009, but the organisation continues local FM broadcasting from these studios in Quito.
status: Working radio studio, not a visitor site; no public museum or tours

name: Cane Ridge Meeting House
address: 1655 Cane Ridge Road, Paris, KY 40361, USA
description: A log meeting house built in 1791, site of the Cane Ridge Revival of August 1801 — the largest camp meeting of the Second Great Awakening, drawing an estimated 20,000 people. Presbyterian, Methodist, and Baptist ministers preached together over several days of continuous open-air services, launching the camp-meeting tradition that spread revival across the American frontier through the 19th century.
status: Open to the public seasonally, April–November; contact the site for current hours

image_source.credit: Chris Light (talk), CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 3.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Jeam-ri 3.1 Independence Movement Memorial Hall
address: 322-4 Jeam-ri, Hyangnam-eup, Hwaseong-si, Gyeonggi-do, South Korea
description: On 15 April 1919, during Japan's crackdown following Korea's March 1st Movement for independence, Japanese soldiers forced about 20 male Christians and Cheondoists into the Jeam-ri Protestant church, locked the doors, and killed them with gunfire and bayonets before burning the building. A joint cemetery for 23 martyrs stands on the hill behind the site, alongside a memorial hall opened in 2001 and a further independence movement memorial hall completed in 2024.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: Frank William Schofield, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: Public domain — no attribution required.
image_source.note: Historical 1919 photograph of the massacre site ("only one house remains intact"), not the modern memorial hall — no free photo of the current building was found.

name: Taiyuan Massacre Memorial — Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception
address: 174 Jiefang Road, Taiyuan, Shanxi 030009, China
description: On 9 July 1900, during the Boxer Rebellion, the governor of Shanxi had 46 foreign missionaries — 34 Protestants and 12 Catholics — massacred in the courtyard of his palace in Taiyuan; by summer's end, an estimated 1,500-2,000 Chinese Christians had also been killed across the province, in what one historian has called the greatest single tragedy in the history of Christian evangelicalism. A Cemetery of the (Catholic) Boxer Martyrs was built in Taiyuan in 1901, and this cathedral — rebuilt after the massacre and restored again for its 2006 centenary — now carries a memorial tablet to the victims.
interest_note: This is a Roman Catholic cathedral, not an evangelical institution, and its surviving memorial commemorates the twelve Catholic priests killed alongside the missionaries. It is included here because the massacre itself was overwhelmingly Protestant in its toll — 34 of the 46 killed at the governor's palace, and the great majority of the roughly 2,000 Chinese Christians killed across Shanxi that summer — and this cathedral is the only substantial surviving memorial marking the event.
status: Open to the public

image_source.credit: 猫猫的日记本, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons
image_source.redistribution_note: CC BY-SA 3.0 — requires attribution if used; not an unrestricted public-domain claim.

name: Immanuel General Mission Headquarters, Tokyo
address: 2-1 Kanda Surugadai, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo, Japan
description: On 26 June 1942, Japanese authorities arrested Holiness pastor David Tsugio Tsutada and roughly 130 other Christians for refusing to bow toward the Imperial Palace and perform mandatory Shinto shrine worship, saying 'only God in heaven is divine. We worship Him alone.' Tsutada spent two years in solitary confinement at Sugamo Prison. After his release, he founded a new church in Tokyo in 1945, naming it 'Immanuel' — 'You, O God, are with us, just as you were in the cell with me.' That congregation grew into the Immanuel General Mission, a Japanese Holiness denomination headquartered here today.
status: Working denominational headquarters
